back: able to continue over time without depleting resources
example: Governments are investing in sustainable energy sources like wind and solar.

back: tending to cause harm or damage
example: Plastic waste is detrimental to marine ecosystems.

back: to make something less severe or harmful
example: Planting trees can help mitigate the effects of climate change.

back: the reduction of something to a critically low level
example: The depletion of natural resources is a pressing global concern.

back: the protection and careful management of nature
example: Wildlife conservation requires international cooperation.

back: present, appearing, or found everywhere
example: Smartphones have become ubiquitous in modern life.

back: a new method, idea, or product
example: Technological innovation has transformed how we communicate.

back: the use of machines to do work previously done by people
example: Automation may displace many manual jobs in the coming decades.

back: the most advanced; at the forefront of development
example: The lab uses cutting-edge equipment for its research.

back: no longer produced or used; out of date
example: Older devices quickly become obsolete as technology advances.

back: the subjects that make up a course of study
example: Critical thinking should be embedded in the school curriculum.

back: memorization through repetition rather than understanding
example: Rote learning is less effective than active engagement with material.

back: considering the whole rather than separate parts
example: A holistic approach to education nurtures both intellect and character.

back: teaching, or the fee charged for it
example: University tuition has risen sharply over the past decade.

back: relating to skills for a particular job or trade
example: Vocational training prepares students for specific careers.

back: involving little physical activity
example: A sedentary lifestyle increases the risk of heart disease.

back: the state of being comfortable, healthy, or happy
example: Regular exercise contributes to mental well-being.

back: a widespread occurrence of a disease in a community
example: Obesity has reached epidemic proportions in some countries.

back: designed to keep something undesirable from occurring
example: Preventive healthcare reduces long-term medical costs.

back: containing substances needed for health and growth
example: Schools should provide nutritious meals for children.

back: an unfair difference in status or opportunity
example: Income inequality has widened in many developed nations.

back: the increasing movement of people into cities
example: Rapid urbanization has strained public infrastructure.

back: showing a great deal of variety
example: Cosmopolitan cities are home to diverse cultures.

back: working from a location outside the office
example: Remote work has reshaped the modern employment landscape.

back: the effectiveness of effort, measured by output
example: Flexible hours can boost employee productivity.

back: no longer needed; (of a worker) dismissed as surplus
example: Hundreds were made redundant when the factory closed.

back: although; even though
example: The plan succeeded, albeit at a significant cost.

back: in spite of; despite
example: Notwithstanding the risks, the company expanded overseas.

back: as a result
example: Demand fell sharply; consequently, prices dropped.

back: to weaken or damage gradually
example: Corruption undermines public trust in institutions.

back: to publicly recommend or support
example: Many experts advocate investing in renewable energy.

back: to make a problem worse
example: Cutting funding would only exacerbate the crisis.

back: to make an action or process easier
example: Good infrastructure facilitates economic growth.

back: widespread in a particular area or time
example: Smartphone addiction is increasingly prevalent among teenagers.

back: evoking strong interest or conviction
example: She made a compelling argument for reform.

back: possible to do easily or conveniently
example: It is not financially feasible to build the bridge this year.

back: certain to happen; unavoidable
example: Some degree of automation in industry seems inevitable.

back: more important than anything else; supreme
example: Safety is of paramount importance on construction sites.

back: real and able to be shown or felt
example: The policy produced tangible improvements in air quality.

back: producing a great deal of profit
example: Software engineering can be a lucrative career.
